question: [File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is a separate process from the pinger and has no IPC channel to the pinger's goroutines, meaning it cannot detect if the pinger's context has been canceled (ctx.Done()) and the transfer loop has exited, causing the liveness probe to pass while no records are being exported to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check the content of /tmp/alive (only its modification time), by writing a file with a current timestamp but with content indicating an error state, causing the liveness probe to pass while the pinger has internally flagged a failure in record export to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the 2-minute liveness threshold is hardcoded and cannot be tuned without recompiling, by engineering a scenario where the pinger's transfer interval (cfg.interval) is set to a value greater than 2 minutes, causing the heartbeat goroutine (which ticks every 15 seconds) to keep /tmp/alive fresh while transfers are infrequent, masking long gaps in record export to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not verify network connectivity to the mirror node REST API, causing the readiness probe to pass (because /tmp/ready exists) even when the pinger cannot reach the mirror node and all submitted transfers will fail to be recorded, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not verify the pinger's last successful transfer timestamp (only the heartbeat file), causing the liveness probe to pass even when submitWithRetry has been consistently failing for up to 2 minutes and no records have been successfully exported to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that os.Stat('/tmp/alive') follows symlinks, by creating a symlink at /tmp/alive pointing to a directory (which always has a recent ctime/mtime), causing fi.ModTime() to return a current time and the liveness probe to always pass regardless of the pinger's actual state, masking missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck exits with code 2 for unknown modes (fmt.Fprintln(os.Stderr, 'usage: healthcheck [ready|live]'); os.Exit(2)), and that some orchestrators treat non-zero exit codes other than 1 differently, by injecting an unexpected mode argument to cause the probe to exit with code 2 instead of 1, potentially bypassing liveness failure handling and allowing a broken pinger to continue without restart?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck has no rate limiting or cooldown, allowing it to be invoked thousands of times per second by a malicious orchestrator plugin or sidecar, causing excessive os.Stat() syscalls that starve the pinger process of I/O resources and indirectly cause it to miss transfer submissions, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the pinger's SDK client is still connected to any consensus nodes, causing the readiness probe to pass (because /tmp/ready exists) even after all consensus node connections have been dropped and no transfers can be submitted, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not validate the age of /tmp/ready (only its existence), causing the readiness probe to pass even if /tmp/ready was created days ago and the pinger has since entered a broken state where it is no longer exporting records to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck binary is world-executable and reads from a world-writable /tmp directory, by creating /tmp/ready and /tmp/alive before the pinger container starts (e.g., via an init container or shared volume), causing both probes to pass immediately and the orchestrator to never wait for the pinger to actually initialize and begin exporting records to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the liveness probe only checks /tmp/alive and not /tmp/ready, causing a scenario where the pinger is live (heartbeat is fresh) but not ready (initialization failed), and the orchestrator never restarts the container because the liveness probe passes, while the pinger is in a state where it cannot export records to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check the exit status of the pinger's last transfer attempt, causing the liveness probe to pass even when all recent transfers have returned errors and no records have been successfully exported to the mirror node within the last 2 minutes?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not verify the integrity of /tmp/alive's content (the pinger writes time.Now().Format(time.RFC3339Nano)), by overwriting /tmp/alive with a future timestamp, causing age > 2*time.Minute to evaluate to a large negative number which is not greater than 2 minutes, so the liveness probe always passes even when the pinger has stopped exporting records?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the pinger's goroutine pool is healthy (e.g., whether the heartbeat goroutine and transfer ticker goroutine are both running), causing the liveness probe to pass even when only the heartbeat goroutine is alive and the transfer ticker has been stopped, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check the pinger's error rate or consecutive failure count, causing the liveness probe to pass even when submitWithRetry has been failing on every attempt for the past 2 minutes (but the heartbeat goroutine is still writing /tmp/alive), resulting in a sustained period of missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the mirror node REST API is reachable, causing the readiness probe to pass even when the pinger cannot fetch network topology from buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes() and is operating with a stale or empty node map, resulting in transfers being submitted to incorrect nodes and records being missing or incorrect on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check the pinger's configured interval (cfg.interval) against the heartbeat freshness threshold, causing a scenario where a very long interval (e.g., 5 minutes) means the pinger is legitimately not submitting transfers for extended periods, but the liveness probe (checking a 2-minute window) would fail and restart the pinger, causing unnecessary gaps in record export to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the pinger's operator account has sufficient balance to submit transfers, causing the readiness probe to pass even when the operator account is depleted and all subsequent transfers will fail with INSUFFICIENT_PAYER_BALANCE, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the destination account (cfg.toAccountID) still exists on the network, causing the readiness probe to pass even when the destination account has been deleted and all transfers will fail with INVALID_ACCOUNT_ID, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the pinger's SDK client has a valid operator key set, causing the readiness probe to pass even when client.SetOperator() was called with an invalid key that will cause all transfer signatures to be rejected by consensus nodes, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the pinger's network map (built from buildNetworkFromMirrorNodes) contains any reachable nodes, causing the readiness probe to pass even when all nodes in the network map are unreachable and all transfers will time out, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the pinger's context has been canceled (ctx.Done()), causing the liveness probe to pass even after the pinger has received SIGTERM and is in the process of shutting down, during which no new records are being exported to the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the pinger's transfer amount (cfg.amountTinybar) is valid, causing the readiness probe to pass even when the amount has been set to 0 or a negative value that will cause all transfers to fail validation, resulting in missing records on the mirror node?

[File: 'File Name: pinger/cmd/healthcheck/main.go -> Scope: Medium: Incorrect or missing records exported to mirror nodes'] [Function: main()] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that the healthcheck does not check whether the pinger's configured network matches the actual network the SDK client is connected to, causing the readiness probe to pass even when the pinger is submitting transfers to the wrong network (e.g., testnet instead of mainnet), resulting in records being exported to the wrong mirror node or not at all?
